title: About
text: Hello! My name is Alonso, a mathematician-in-training based in Texas (and soon, Mexico City). I have been fascinated by mathematics since childhood, and I went on to obtain an undergraduate degree in the subject. However, for various reasons, I have been away from mathematics for several years. I am now making my way back to it with renewed enthusiasm and curiosity. This blog is a way for me to share my journey back into mathematics, as well as scattered reflections on the philosophy and pedagogy of the subject. I hope that my reflections and explorations will be useful and inspiring to others who are also interested in mathematics, whether they are students, educators, or simply curious minds.

title: Pathological Periodicity, Part I: Introduction and Preliminaries
text:
It is an elementary exercise in linear algebra to show that the set of all periodic functions is not a vector space, since the sum of periodic functions with incommensurable periods (i.e., periods whose ratio is irrational) may fail to be periodic. However, this leads to many more subtle questions. For instance:

Do there exist any periodic functions with incommensurable periods whose sum is nonetheless periodic? Can we characterize such functions?
Constant functions are trivially periodic with any real period. Does there exist a non-constant periodic function with 2 or more incommensurable periods? If so, does there exist such a function that is not constant almost everywhere?
Which subsets of periodic functions are vector spaces? Can any such vector space contain two or more functions with incommensurable periods?
How do the answers to these questions change when considering various families of periodic functions, such as continuous, strictly periodic, measurable functions, etc.?

As it turns out, these seemingly simple questions open a can of worms involving many fields of mathematics at once: algebra, measure and ergodic theory, topology of \(\mathbb{R}\), and the foundations of mathematics (namely, set theory and the Axiom of Choice). In particular, using the Axiom of Choice on quotients \(\mathbb{R}/G\), where \(G\) is either a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-submodule of \(\mathbb{R}\) or a \(\mathbb{Q}\)-subspace of \(\mathbb{R}\), we can find highly pathological examples answering the first three questions in the affirmative, but only for non-measurable functions. This is all very closely related to what is called the Periodic Decomposition Problem, about which much has been written (see for instance this paper).
I am working now on figuring out 2 main questions: first, whether the existence of these pathological periodic functions is equivalent to or strictly weaker than the Axiom of Choice (I suspect the latter), and second, just how large the dimension of a vector space of periodic functions can be when it contains functions with incommensurable periods (I suspect not very large). I will write up my findings on these questions more formally in the next few months. However, in the meantime, I want to use this Pathological Periodicity series in the blog to further motivate this line of inquiry and record some elementary proofs I have come up with of classic results related to these questions, but that I won’t be including in the final write-up itself, since they are not new. I hope that these elementary proofs will be particularly useful to fellow students of mathematics as well as to instructors, and that they will inspire others to explore these questions further.

title: Pathological Periodicity, Part I: Introduction and Preliminaries
text:
2. Preliminaries
Let’s review some basic definitions and facts about periodic functions that set the stage for our further explorations and that start to reveal the interplay of algebra, topology, and analysis in the world of periodicity.
Definition 2.1 (Periodic Function) A function \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) is said to be periodic if there exists a non-zero real number \(p\) such that \(f(x+p)=f(x)\) for all \(x\in\mathbb{R}\). If there exists a smallest positive such \(p\), it is called the fundamental period of \(f\), and we say \(f\) is strictly periodic.
Remark: Note that a function can be periodic without being strictly periodic; for instance, the constant functions are periodic with any real period, but they are not strictly periodic. Essentially, the whole line of inquiry that we are following in the first series of this blog is about the existence of non-strictly periodic functions other than the constant functions, in particular those with incommensurable periods.
Definition 2.2 (The Period Module) For any function \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\), we denote by \(P_f\) the set of all periods of \(f\), i.e., \(P_f := \{p\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\} : f(x+p)=f(x) \text{ for all } x\in\mathbb{R}\}\). We call \(P_f\) the period module of \(f\), for reasons that will become clear in a moment. Also, we say \(f, g\) are incommensurable if \(P_f\cap P_g = \{0\}\), and they are commensurable otherwise. Note constant functions are trivially commensurable with any periodic function, since they are periodic with any period. And note that non-periodic functions still have a period module, but it is just \(\{0\}\).
Definition 2.3 (\(\mathbb{Z}\)-submodule and its dimension) A subset \(M\) of \(\mathbb{R}\) is called a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-submodule if it is closed under addition and under multiplication by integers. The dimension of a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-submodule \(M\) is the cardinality of a maximal linearly independent subset of \(M\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\).
Definition 2.4 (\(\mathbb{Q}\)-subspace and Period Space) For any function \(f\), we can consider the linear span of its period module over the rational numbers \(\mathbb{Q}\). We denote this by \(V_f := \text{span}_\mathbb{Q}(P_f)\), and call it the period space of \(f\). By definition, \(V_f\) is a \(\mathbb{Q}\)-subspace of \(\mathbb{R}_{\mathbb{Q}}\), which is how we denote the vector space of \(\mathbb{R}\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\).
We immediately find some basic facts about periodic functions:

Fact 0: If \(f, g\) are incommensurable functions, any (nonzero) periods must be incommensurable real numbers. This is because if \(p\in P_f\) and \(q\in P_g\) are such that \(p/q\in\mathbb{Q}\), then there exist integers \(n, m\) such that \(np = mq\), and thus \(np\in P_f\cap P_g\), showing that \(f\) and \(g\) are not incommensurable functions.
Fact 1: If \(f\) is periodic with period \(p\), then it is also periodic with respect to any integer linear combinations of periods of \(f\). This means \(P_f\) is a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-submodule of \(\mathbb{R}\).
Fact 2: If \(f\) is strictly periodic with fundamental period \(p\), then the set of all periods of \(f\) is precisely the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module \(p\mathbb{Z}:= \{np : n\in\mathbb{Z}\}\). I.e., \(P_f\) is necessarily a one-dimensional submodule.
Fact 3: The dimension of the period module \(P_f\) as a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module is not necessarily the same as the dimension of the period space \(V_f\) as a \(\mathbb{Q}\)-vector space. For instance, if \(P_f = \mathbb{Q}\), its dimension as a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module is infinite, but its dimension as a \(\mathbb{Q}\)-subspace (\(V_f = \mathbb{Q}\)) is exactly 1.
Fact 4: If \(f\) is continuous and periodic, then it is either constant or strictly periodic. This can be shown as follows: For a given \(x,y\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(f(y)\neq f(x)\), continuity of \(f\) implies that \(y^\prime=\inf\{z>x\mid f(z)=f(y)\}>x\) and \(f(y^\prime)=f(y)\). Thus the interval \([x, y^\prime]\) cannot contain any full period of \(f\), and thus \(y^\prime-x\) must be a positive number less than any positive period of \(f\). Hence the infimum \(p\) of positive periods must be greater than zero. If \(p\) itself were not a period, we could find two distinct periods arbitrarily close to \(p\), and their difference would be a period smaller than \(p\), violating the infimum. Thus \(p\) is the fundamental period of \(f\), and \(f\) is strictly periodic.

Now recall the definition of a dense set:
Definition 2.5 (Dense Set) A subset \(S\) of \(\mathbb{R}\) is said to be dense if for every open interval \((a,b)\), there exists an element of \(S\) in that interval. Equivalently, the closure of \(S\) is \(\mathbb{R}\).
We are now ready to see our first result about periodic functions with incommensurable periods, which relates the topology of period sets to their algebraic structure:
Proposition 2.6: If \(p, q\) are incommensurable (\(p/q\not\in \mathbb{Q}\)), then the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module \(\mathbb{Z}_{p,q}:=\{np + mq : n,m\in\mathbb{Z}\}\) is dense in \(\mathbb{R}\). In particular, if a function \(f\) has two incommensurable periods \(p\) and \(q\), then it is periodic with respect to every element of \(\mathbb{Z}_{p,q}\), and thus has a dense set of periods and is not strictly periodic. Therefore the only such functions that are continuous are constant functions.
Proof: To see the first part of the proposition, we make use of the fact that irrationals can be approximated by rationals very nicely: by Dirichlet’s approximation theorem, we know that for any \(\epsilon>0\), we can find integers \(-n, m\) such that \(|mp/q -(-n)|<\epsilon/|q|\), and thus \(|mp +nq|<\epsilon\). This shows all intervals containing the origin have elements of \(\mathbb{Z}_{p,q}\). Now take any interval \((a, b)\). By the foregoing, we can find \(x,y\in\mathbb{Z}_{p,q}\) with \(|x|\leq|a|\), and \[0<|y|<\min\left(\{|x-b|, |x-a|, |b-a|\}\setminus{\{0\}}\right)\] Where we remove \(0\) in case \(x=a\). There thus must be some integer \(n\) so that \(x+ny\in (a, b)\). But by Fact 1, \(x+ny\) is also a period of \(f\). Thus \(\mathbb{Z}_{p,q}\) is dense in \(\mathbb{R}\). The second part of the proposition follows immediately from the first part and from the previous facts.
We want to conclude this section with a corollary that shows how the existence of incommensurable periods prevents the sum of continuous periodic functions from being periodic, and thus prevents any set of continuous periodic functions containing two or more incommensurable functions from being a vector space. We will see later that this is not the case for non-continuous functions.
For this, we need the following definition which will prove useful throuhgout this series:
Definition 2.7 (Difference Operator) For any function \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) and any \(p\in\mathbb{R}\), we define the difference operator in p \(\Delta_p\) by \(\Delta_p f(x) := f(x+p) - f(x)\) for all \(x\in\mathbb{R}\). As the reader can verify, these are linear operators for all \(p\), and they commute with each other, i.e., \(\Delta_p\Delta_q = \Delta_q\Delta_p\) for all \(p, q\in\mathbb{R}\).
This operator measures how much \(f\) changes when we translate by \(p\). In particular, \(\Delta_p f\) is identically zero if and only if \(p\) is a period of \(f\). Moreover, whenever \(q \not\in P_f\), the nature of \(\Delta_q f\) and its period module determines much about the relationship between \(P_f\) and \(P_{f+g}\) for any function \(g\) with period \(q\), as we will see in the next proposition:
Proposition 2.8: For any \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) and any \(q\in\mathbb{R}\), \(P_f\subseteq P_{\Delta_q f}\). Thus if \(\Delta_q f\) has any periods that are incommensurable with periods of \(f\), \(P_{\Delta_q f}\) is dense and has dimension at least 2. Moroever, given incommensurable functions \(f,g\), if \(f+g\) is periodic, then \(P_f\) is a proper submodule of \(P_{\Delta_q f}\) for all \(q\in P_g\). The same holds for \(P_g\) and \(P_{\Delta_p g}\) whenever \(p\in P_f\).
Proof. The first part of the proposition follows immediately from the definition of \(\Delta_q f\) and the fact that \(P_f\) is a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module. For the second part, if \(g\) is incommensurable with \(f\) and \(f+g\) is periodic (say, with period \(r\)), then for any \(q\in P_g\), \(\Delta_r (f+g) = 0 = \Delta_q g\) and \[0 = \Delta_q\Delta_r(f+g) = \Delta_r\Delta_q f\] showing that \(r\) is a period of \(\Delta_q f\). But \(r\) must be incommensurable with all periods of \(f\) because otherwise \(g\) would not be incommensurable with \(f\), showing \(P_f\) is a proper submodule of \(P_{\Delta_q f}\). The same argument works for \(P_g\) and \(P_{\Delta_p g}\).
For now, we conclude with the following corrollary, which is a direct consequence of the previous propositions and which we leave as an exercise to the reader (hint: use the difference operator, and the boundedness of continuous periodic functions, and the fact that the period module of a continuous periodic function fails to be one-dimensional if and only if it is constant):
Corollary 2.9: Let \(f, g\) be continuous periodic functions. Then \(f+g\) is periodic if and only if \(f, g\) are commensurable. Thus no set of continuous periodic functions containing two or more incommensurable functions can be a vector space.
Remark: This corollary says essentially that no incommensurable continuous functions satisfy the necesary condition in the second part of Proposition 2.8, naturally raising the question of whether any incommensurable functions can satisfy this condition. As we will see in the next section, the answer is yes, and we can even find strictly periodic (but not continuous) functions satisfying this condition. Moreover, the condition is not only necessary but also sufficient for the sum of incommensurable functions to be periodic, assuming the Axiom of Choice. This is deeply tied to the Periodic Decomposition Problem mentioned earlier, which we will explore in more detail in future posts.

title: Pathological Periodicity, Part I: Introduction and Preliminaries
text:
3. Some non-continuous periodic functions
Most of the time, when we think of periodic functions, we think of nice, well-behaved functions like sines and cosines. From the perspective of their period modules, we have shown these are all rather boring: they are all one-dimensional over \(\mathbb{Z}\), none of them are dense, and any vector spaces \(\mathcal V\) consisting solely of strictly periodic functions cannot contain any incommensurable functions, meaning the period spaces \(V_f\) for all \(f\in \mathcal V\) live along a single 1-dimensional line \(p\mathbb{Q}\) of \(\mathbb{R}\).
But, as alluded to many times already, we can find more interesting periodic functions when we drop continuity:
Examples 3.1: Let \(p, q\) be incommensurable, and consider the functions \(\mathbb{1}_{\mathbb{Q}}\), \(\mathbb{1}_{\mathbb{Z}_{p,q}}\), and \[
   \begin{split}
    &f(x) =
    \begin{cases}
         0 & \text{if } x \not\in \mathbb{Z}_{p,q}\\
        n & \text{if } x = mp+nq\in \mathbb{Z}_{p,q}
    \end{cases} \\ &\text{ and } \\
    &g(x) =
    \begin{cases}
        0 & \text{if } x \not\in \mathbb{Z}_{p,q}\\
        -m & \text{if } x = mp+nq\in \mathbb{Z}_{p,q}
    \end{cases}
\end{split}
\]
Notice \(\mathbb{1}_{\mathbb{Q}}\) has a period module equal to \(\mathbb{Q}\), which is dense and has infinite (actually, uncountable) dimension as a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module, but its period space \(V_f\) has dimension 1 over \(\mathbb{Q}\).
Meanwhile, \(\mathbb{1}_{\mathbb{Z}_{p,q}}\) has a period module equal to \(\mathbb{Z}_{p,q}\), which is also dense and has dimension 2 as a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module (and its period space \(V_f\) also has dimension 2 over \(\mathbb{Q}\)).
As for \(f\) and \(g\), note \(P_f = p\mathbb{Z}\) and \(P_g = q\mathbb{Z}\). They are strictly periodic and incommensurable. However, evaluating \((f+g)(x)\) for \(x = mp+nq\) yields \(n-m\). If we translate by \(p+q\), we get \((m+1)p + (n+1)q\), which maps to \((n+1)-(m+1) = n-m\). Thus, \(P_{f+g} = (p+q)\mathbb{Z}\). They are incommensurable, strictly periodic functions whose sum is strictly periodic!
Notice however that these examples are constant almost everywhere, and all have finite dimensional period spaces (even when the period module is infinite dimensional). Also notice that \(\alpha f + \beta g\) is periodic if and only if \(\alpha/\beta\in \mathbb{Q}\). This immediately leads to at least three more questions:
Questions:

Must all periodic functions with the above behaviour be constant a.e.?
What is the maximum dimension a period space can have?
Is there any pair of incommensurable functions whose linear span over \(\mathbb{R}\) consists solely of periodic functions (i.e. \(\alpha f + \beta g\) is periodic for all \(\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}\))?

In the next installment of Pathological Periodicity, we will first use the axiom of choice to find a periodic function with a dense period module that is constant only on sets of measure zero. We will then prove that no such functions can be measurable, a classic result in measure theory. The third installment of the series will then explore period modules and spaces in more detail, and tackle the question about the maximal dimension of period spaces. Finally, the fourth installment will more explicitly tie all we have done to the Periodic Decomposition Problem, and will tackle the third of the above questions. Stay tuned!

title: Welcome to Mathematical Adventures!
text:
Welcome to Mathematical Adventures! After many years away from mathematics, I am finally making my way back to this beautiful subject. In this blog, I will first and foremost share my reflections on mathematical topics as I dive into exploring (and re-learning) them. I hope to also provide some meta-mathematical reflections touching on philosophy, pedagogy, and the travails of becoming a mathematician in the current social, political, and economic context.
When it comes to the mathematical content of this blog, I hope to step away from the highly polished, often opaque final forms of classical theorems and actually get our hands dirty. We will explore rigorous mathematics with the mindset of an adventurer: taking the scenic routes, avoiding black-box machinery whenever possible, and building our intuition from the ground up. I think this approach is not only more fun, but also more rewarding in terms of deep understanding, especially for anyone like myself needing to re-learn the basics.
This blog will contain some stand alone posts, and some series. The first series I am launching, Pathological Periodicity, explores the structure of the sets of periods of periodic functions \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R},\) the pathologies that arise within the set of periodic functions whenever we assume the axiom of choice, and related matters. Hop on over to the first post to get started!
